---
title: "ivcheck"
subtitle: "Tests of IV validity in R"
author: "Charles Coverdale"
institute: "London, United Kingdom"
date: "2026-04-20"
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"

format:
  beamer:
    aspectratio: 169
    fontsize: 11pt
    navigation: empty
    theme: default
    colortheme: default
    fonttheme: default
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    include-in-header: header.tex
    slide-level: 2
    keep-tex: false
    fig-align: center
    classoption:
      - t
---

## The question

\vspace{1.5em}

\begin{center}
\Large\bfseries\color{econblue}
Is this instrumental variable doing the job?
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}

\begin{center}
\normalsize\itshape
Empirical IV papers routinely estimate a LATE and move on. The
identifying assumptions are testable, yet the tests are almost
never reported.
\end{center}

::: notes
Open on the question, not the package. The LATE framework makes two
empirical commitments: exclusion and monotonicity. Both are testable.
Almost no applied paper runs the test. 30 seconds.
:::


## Why it matters

- **Applied econometricians**: IV designs are used by every top-5 journal, yet fewer than 5 percent of published papers report a falsification test
- **Referees**: limited tooling makes the test hard to ask for, even when the design cries out for it
- **Methodologists**: three landmark tests exist (2015, 2017, 2023) but live only as author-hosted Stata and Matlab scripts
- **Replication**: published falsification claims cannot be reproduced without porting the tests by hand

::: notes
Name the stakeholders. Emphasise: the tests exist, the gap is tooling.
Bring the referee point out loud: easier to ask if it takes one line.
60 seconds.
:::


## What is already out there

- **`fixest`, `ivreg`**: estimate IV models, but do not test validity\footnote{\scriptsize Berg\'e (2018), \textit{fixest}; Kleiber \& Zeileis (2020), \textit{ivreg}.}
- **`ivDiag`** (Lal, Lockhart, Xu, Zu 2024): first-stage F, weak-IV, LATE bounds, partial robustness; does not implement the LATE-validity tests
- **Stata `testjfe`** (Frandsen, BYU 2020): FLL test only; no R equivalent
- **Author replication archives**: per-paper Matlab, Gauss, Stata; not packaged

\vspace{0.5em}

The gap: **no single R package runs the published falsification tests on a fitted IV model.**

::: notes
Respectful on existing packages. The gap is about falsification-test
coverage, not about IV estimation. 45 seconds.
:::


## What ivcheck offers

1. **Coverage**: three published tests plus a one-shot wrapper that inspects a fitted IV model and runs every applicable test
2. **Dispatch**: S3 methods on `fixest::feols` and `ivreg::ivreg` objects, so the tests drop in after an existing IV estimation
3. **Fidelity**: variance-weighted Kitagawa, full CLR with adaptive moment selection for Mourifi\'e-Wan, chi-squared asymptotic plus bootstrap for FLL

\vspace{0.5em}

Source on GitHub, CRAN submission in progress\footnote{\scriptsize Coverdale (2026), \textit{ivcheck: Tests of IV Validity in R}, available at github.com/charlescoverdale/ivcheck.}.

::: notes
Three selling points. Emphasise the wrapper: user does not have to
know which test applies. Dispatch makes it composable with existing
workflows. 45 seconds.
:::


## Three testable implications

\vspace{0.3em}

**Kitagawa (2015)** \hfill for discrete Z, binary D:
\begin{equation}
\sup_{y \leq y'}\, \big[P(Y \in [y, y'], D = d \mid Z = z_L) - P(Y \in [y, y'], D = d \mid Z = z_H)\big]^{+}
\end{equation}

**Mourifi\'e-Wan (2017)** \hfill same null, conditional on covariates:
\begin{equation}
E[m(W;\ \theta) \mid X] \leq 0 \text{ for all } X
\end{equation}

**Frandsen-Lefgren-Leslie (2023)** \hfill judge-IV designs, `K` judges:
\begin{equation}
T_n = \sum_j n_j (\hat\mu_j - \hat\alpha - \hat\beta \hat p_j)^2 / \hat\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{K-2}
\end{equation}

::: notes
Three equations, three tests. Do not read them out. Point at the
positive-part KS for Kitagawa, the CLR conditional moment inequality
for MW, and the overidentification chi-squared for FLL. 60 seconds.
:::


## Package architecture

::: columns
::: {.column width="58%"}

Core exports:

- **`iv_kitagawa()`**: binary D, discrete Z
- **`iv_mw()`**: same, plus covariate path
- **`iv_testjfe()`**: judge-IV design
- **`iv_check()`**: wrapper, runs all applicable
- **`iv_power()`**: Monte Carlo power curves

Dispatch: `fixest`, `ivreg`. Deps: `cli`, `stats`, `parallel`.

:::
::: {.column width="40%"}

\begin{block}{Uniform output}
Every test returns an \texttt{iv\_test} object:

\texttt{statistic} | \texttt{p\_value} | \texttt{binding}

{\small Print, plot, summary methods; composes with \texttt{modelsummary}.}
\end{block}

:::
:::

::: notes
Two-column layout shows exports and schema at once. Emphasise the
iv_check one-shot pattern. 45 seconds.
:::


## Minimal working example

```r
library(ivcheck)
library(fixest)

data(card1995)
m <- feols(
  lwage ~ age + black + south | college ~ near_college,
  data = card1995
)

iv_check(m, n_boot = 500)
```

\footnotesize
One line after the IV estimation. `iv_check()` inspects the model,
detects a binary treatment and a binary instrument, runs Kitagawa and
Mourifi\'e-Wan, and returns a tidy verdict table.

::: notes
Live-read the code. Emphasise one line. No arguments beyond the
model and the bootstrap budget. 45 seconds.
:::


## Case study: Card (1995) proximity to college

**Data.** 3{,}003 observations from the National Longitudinal Survey of Young Men (Card 1995), distributed on CRAN via the `wooldridge` package.

**Question.** *Does the standard college / near-college IV satisfy the LATE validity conditions on the discretised treatment?*

**Why this case.** Canonical applied IV example. Tested by Kitagawa (2015) and replicated by Mourifi\'e-Wan (2017), both rejecting on the discretised form\footnote{\scriptsize Kitagawa (2015), Table 2; Mourifi\'e-Wan (2017), Table 1.}. Our replication should reproduce the rejection.

::: notes
Setup slide. No results yet. Name the data, the question, why it is
the right test case. Point forward to the bootstrap distribution.
45 seconds.
:::


## Card rejects: Kitagawa statistic 5.25, p < 0.01

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.68\textheight]{figures/fig-card-bootstrap.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.2em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Bootstrap null distribution of the Kitagawa statistic
on Card (1995), N = 3{,}003. Observed statistic (red line) sits far in
the upper tail: p-value below 0.01 at 500 bootstrap draws. Binding
interval \textbf{[6.25, 7.78]} on log wage, d = 0 direction. Source:
Card (1995), NLS of Young Men; replication via \texttt{ivcheck::card1995}.}
```

::: notes
The money slide. 90 seconds. Walk through: red vertical line is the
observed statistic, histogram is the bootstrap null under the tested
IV-validity assumption. The observed value sits in the extreme upper
tail. Binding interval tells us the violation is at the upper end of
the log wage distribution, in the d = 0 direction.
:::


## FLL asymptotic distribution matches Monte Carlo

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.68\textheight]{figures/fig-testjfe-null.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.2em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Empirical null distribution of \texttt{iv\_testjfe}
at K = 20 judges and N = 3{,}000 over 200 replications. Histogram is
the empirical T\_n density; red curve is the asymptotic $\chi^2_{18}$.
Test is mildly conservative (empirical size 1.5 percent at nominal
5 percent); the bootstrap option trims this to 2.5 percent.}
```

::: notes
Diagnostic slide. 60 seconds. Empirical agreement with chi-squared in
the bulk; slight compression in the tail comes from estimated p_j
entering as a regressor, a classical errors-in-variables effect.
Recommend the bootstrap method for publication-grade p-values at
modest n_j.
:::


## What ivcheck does not yet do

- **Continuous instruments**: discretise into quantile bins for now; Andrews-Shi continuous-Z extension in v0.2.0
- **Fuzzy regression discontinuity**: has its own test (Arai et al. 2022); `iv_frd()` deferred to v0.2.0
- **Fixed-effects IV models**: aborts with a clear message; pre-demean within FE cells and call the default method
- **Stata cross-validation**: numerical agreement with Frandsen's `testjfe` and Chernozhukov et al.\ `clrtest` pending

\vspace{0.5em}

**v0.2.0**: `iv_frd()`, continuous-Z path, multivariate-x in `iv_mw`, tensor-product basis, FLL bounded-slope moment inequalities.

::: notes
Honest limitations. Frame the roadmap as a commitment. 45 seconds.
:::


## Contact, code, paper

::: columns
::: {.column width="62%"}

```{=latex}
{\large\bfseries Charles Coverdale\par}
\vspace{0.5em}
\texttt{charles.f.coverdale@gmail.com}\\
\texttt{github.com/charlescoverdale/ivcheck}\\
\texttt{charlescoverdale.github.io/publications}
\vspace{0.9em}

\textbf{Acknowledgements.} Thanks to Toru Kitagawa, Ismael Mourifi\'e, Yuanyuan Wan, Brigham Frandsen, Lars Lefgren, and Emily Leslie for the methodology and publicly-available replication archives. Card (1995) data via the \textsc{CRAN} \texttt{wooldridge} package.
```

:::
::: {.column width="35%"}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/qrcode_paper.png}

\vspace{0.3em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Scan for paper PDF}
\end{center}

:::
:::

::: notes
Close cleanly. Name, email, GitHub, publications URL. QR code points
to the publications page. Open for questions.
:::
